result:
Rain taps against the high loft windows in a rhythm somewhere between a warning and a lullaby. Outside, the city’s sodium lamps stripe the slick streets in duplicated constellations, colors that have never once appeared in nature but nevertheless stand in for warmth when the air is cold enough to sharpen itself against skin. Inside, the kettle begins its climbing hiss that always reminds me of a late-night train entering some mountain tunnel on the other side of the planet—a place I’ve pictured without ever visiting, a place where the air tastes of coal and cedars and somebody’s fresh bread. Steam belly-dances upward; I watch, half hypnotized, because I promised myself an undirected, un-weaponized twenty-five hundred words. Nothing argued into submission, nothing leveraged into proving I am smart or good or even likable. Just a lungful of evening turned inside out on the page.

Still, even sincerity is a kind of performance in a culture that likes to screenshot every sunset for the sake of “later,” which almost never comes. So I’ll say the first true thing I can think of and let the next ride its tail like车厢 couplings. I am tired of the posture of completeness, the curated résumés of the self that we hand out as though personality were a stationary set of finished tasks check-boxes: Has opinions on oat milk—check; rock-climbs—check; credit score in the 700s—check. Beneath those summaries an unspoken crowd presses its face against the glass, begging to walk out into the weather and feel something unrelated to capital.

I keep sipping rusty-colored lapsang tea because it smells the way memory tastes. My mother brewed it on late October afternoons when the power company cut off the lights and her grief could finally unfold without electronic interference. Candle nubs guttered. Shadows played eviction on the walls. She told stories of exile, of uncles who never came back from wars we didn’t learn about in school. I wrapped my hands around the cup, pretending it was a miniature hearth. Every sip scalded, but the outside world looked a little less arctic after each sting.

Now I’m one strand of DNA older than she was then, and every late-October wind knocks something loose in me like a draftsman’s eraser smudging its own charcoal ghosts. I pay the electricity bill on time these days—autopay, technically, the dream of forgetting the mechanics of staying alive—so the lights glimmer despite storms. Yet the candles still come out onto the sill, not out of necessity but ceremony. Flame is a drum the heart’s animal remembers how to follow.

Speaking of animals: for six months I co-parented a stray cat with the neighbor two doors down. She shelter-hopped for a living, the neighbor, switching between battered-women housing, temporary couches, the occasional shelter too overcrowded to ask questions. She called the cat Taxi because its primary hobby was leaving. On full-moon nights it leapt into oblivion across rooftops we didn’t know connected. Every morning she knocked on my door, eyes red with sleeplessness (hers, mine, the moon’s), asking if I’d seen him. We formed a loose alliance: I brought kibble from the coworking vending machine stash, she brought desperation in reusable Tupperware. Eventually Taxi quit returning altogether. Months later I heard from someone who’d heard from someone else that a vet clinic across town had a long-haired tabby with the same missing notch in the left ear. The chip read “Taxi.” The phone number went to voicemail, then later to “no longer in service.” I pictured the woman asleep on a Greyhound heading west, three paperbacks for a pillow. Sometimes choosing vanishing for yourself is the only generous act left.

We are all citizens of vanishings now: half-download keepsakes left in cloud graveyards, unsent messages eroding in draft folders, Spotify playlists for lovers whose names have dragged themselves through so many linguistic iterations they could be characters in adjacent myths. I still have an entire archive of passwords that summon nothing except the ache of their former exclusivity—secret strings of numbers once shared in bed, now hoarded by death and database breaches.

Yesterday I found a cassette in the bottom of a shoebox labeled “correspondence.” My name is inscribed on the spine in adolescent biro ink—round, hopeful characters, the a shaped like an @ symbol long before most of us knew what the internet meant. I had no tape deck anymore, so I walked ten blocks to the anarchist infoshop that doubles as a lending library for obsolete media. A volunteer wearing magenta coveralls threaded the ribbon for me in the back room where incense lurks like an introvert. When he pressed play, an entire eighth-grade afternoon unspooled: I’m twelve, wearing an oversized Nirvana shirt that belonged to my best friend who would, in three years’ time, jump from the upper level of a parking garage after learning the father he never met had just died in a country neither of us had heard of.

On tape I squeak out a love song for a girl whose name has become the punch line of an inside joke with myself. We had no chorus, harmony, or language of desire yet. What I offered instead was pure vowel, open throat recordings of my own future daydreams. Listening to that boy-ghost, I felt both embarrassment and tenderness—that moment when tenderness is embarrassment, and vice versa. Magenta-coveralls lifted an eyebrow like, Do you want this? I nodded, waited till after-hours traffic hummed, then walked home with my own lost voice tucked inside a paper bag smelling of sandalwood and mildewed zines.

I returned to the loft around midnight, dropped the tape on the new coffee table where bills and quarter-life landmarks wait their next category. The city birds were arguing over territories of silence. I slipped out of my boots without untying them—a small rebellion against adult efficiency—and stood on the cold floorboards in socked feet, letting the chill crawl up like a memo from the earth: You are borrowing time at interest rates you cannot predict.

Sometimes I consider the possibility that architecture is just the persistence of bad habits made stone. Every wall started as some ancestor’s knee-jerk reaction to invasion. Towers are anxiety stacked vertically. Suburbia fears poverty arranged in cul-de-sacs. Meanwhile we still cook rice the way grandmothers did on riverbanks under constellations now renamed for marketing teams. Same steam, different anxiety. In college I visited Hiroshima and hiked up to a temple bell still bruised green by radiation. I learned you could hear metal scream a century later if you stood perfectly still and did not blink. On the train home my phone pulsed with ads for discount flights, as though the algorithm understood redemption packages better than any human.

Speaking of algorithms: I dated one once. (Almost.) It was technically a dating-app match whose profile claimed she was “half code, half manifesto.” In person she was a cyborg poet working on a dissertation about surveillance futures. Her room reeked of solder and citrus rind. LED strips under the shelves flashed hex colors at six-minute intervals whose pattern, she claimed, sampled subway mood lighting in Seoul. When she spoke, I watched her irises toggle between brown and luminous ibis-blue—the tint of contact lenses synced to her biofeedback: high heart rate, blue; low, brown. We spent one rain-soaked evening arguing about whether empathy could be crowdsourced. She believed in versions. I clung to the root. After the third disagreement, she tilted her head, toggled purple, and whispered, “How do you know your sadness isn’t just a scraped API endpoint?” Which is either incredibly heartless or the most honest breakup line I’ve ever received. The next morning I unfollowed but did not delete, in case one miraculous day version 2.0 prints out nostalgia like a receipt.

Now I cook for one on a skillet small enough to fit into a hobo’s bindle. The ritual involves too much salt, too little garlic, and podcasts on archival soundscapes recorded underwater, because I like voices that have been filtered through thresholds inhospitable to lungs. Each recipe is an act of insubordination against nutrition trackers and the tyranny of macros. Once I made a stew so dense with black beans the pot resembled a night sky spilled into cookware. I swallowed spoonfuls of galactic broth, pretending the entire cosmos wanted to be digested and loved back into motion.

On the sill sits the colony of succulents I forget for weeks until guilt arrives like a sudden ex. They thrive on neglect—some directive from desert ancestry. They flower once every millennium, perhaps; maybe they have already bloomed and I missed the single trumpet-shaped euphoria. I say good morning out loud anyway, because I read somewhere acknowledgment reduces plant stress, and anyhow I like how words echo dew-less in the loft before the street cafés grind their morning guilt beans.

When loneliness arrives too well-informed, I walk to the river. The boardwalk glitters with crushed mussel shells and tourist coins. Lovers carve oaths into wood already scarred by centuries of salt rot. I trace fingertip over “N 4 V forever,” wondering who N was and if V returned. The river answers in turbid syllables, a dialect older than alphabets. Some days it hawks industrial foam; some days it dawns opal and innocent. I sit on the railing, dangling Vans over thin water-striped void, promising myself I won’t leap if only because the splash would inconvenience the ferry pilots. It’s the small self-concessions that keep collections of carbon from blowing apart. At dusk the warehouses across the way turn lavender, then bruise. Every window squares the sun into postcards no one receives until centuries after fire expires.

When I was seven my father—briefly back from deployment—took me to a planetarium to see a laser Floyd show. He said nothing the entire time, just held my hand as constellations morphed into prism wolves chasing prism deer in three impossible dimensions. I felt, rather than heard, the bass flatten my ribs. Afterward we bought one pink carnation from the vendor outside because they’d run out of red. On the bus home the petals clocked every pothole like a metronome. Back in the house he placed it in an empty jar that once held dill pickle spears and confessed that the name of the dead crewmate he never spoke aloud was etched on the inside of his wedding ring. Then he went to wash engine grease from cuticles that never fully recovered. The carnation lasted four days. I kept the jar and now use it for loose jasmine.

Memory obeys no conservation laws. You can summon jasmine in the middle of winter without violating thermodynamics. You can thumbnail-sketch a morning thirty-three years old and hand it to strangers on the internet. Yet every time we do, we’re scattering pollen into a hurricane named Present. I consider whether each retelling is degradation or transformation, but maybe the dichotomy is the true degradation.

There is a particular sweetness in arriving at the laundromat just late enough that only one dryer remains, quarter-slots already fed by some invisible benefactor. Ten free minutes: windfall currency for the perpetually budgeting. I watched my socks revolve inside the porthole like astronauts practicing slow grief. Overhead a fluorescent bulb had throttled down to sinister migraine strobe. Everyone else avoided that corner, so I carried my basket beneath itature’s stage lighting and read the labels on soap bottles like they were koans: Cold Water Protects Darker Colors. It Feels Like Sunday. Who invents these mantras? I picture a room of failed poets now sublimating grief through branding: the ultimate workshop where each stanza is pre-approved for allergen-free earth-impact market metrics.

Eventually the benefactor’s quarter cavalry ran out with three minutes left. Socks still damp, I fed two of my own coins because fabric softener commercials insist life is unbearable without inner plush. This is how capitalism channels tenderness: make tenderness impossible to afford, then sell it back at low APR.

Back upstairs, the alley cats are quarreling in minor keys. Their yowls carry through thin plaster like soprano circular saws. I pinch the spiral notebook in which I jot sentences deemed unsocialized enough for any public forum: “I sleep better now that I no longer believe the sky is a ceiling.” Or: “My mirror keeps asking to be forgiven for reflecting yesterday without consent.” They sit patient between grocery lists and the specs for friend texted me for a raspberry-pi home fermentation monitor. Tiny revolutions under a rubber-band siege.

Once, I tried to mail my future self a letter—part time-capsule, part apology. I wrote on onion paper, sealed it in wax marbled with turmeric, went to the post office where the woman behind the counter informed me the furthest future delivery offered was six months. Imagine: even futurity has bureaucratic limits. I filled out the yellow form, paid twelve quarters, and walked out feeling like I had arranged my own funeral rehearsal. Six months later, on a Tuesday indistinguishable from the others, the letter arrived while I hunched over the toilet lid replacing the screws. I slit the seal with a screwdriver. Past-me had written, sincerely, to forgive Present-me for whatever debts remained after late-stage capitalism implodes. Reading that almost broke me in a good way, like the time I found polyester frogs stitched to childhood pajama pants in a cousin’s attic, still smelling faintly of bubble gum and pine sap. We look to evidence that time can in fact be non-linear; sometimes it accommodates through paper enzymes and scented permanence.

Speaking of permanence: the tenant above me murders out-of-tune cellos at 11:42 p.m. every night. He claims the building’s landlord allows it because he is curing himself of “cello-anxiety,” a term he invented during a Zoom court hearing for noise complaints. “Confront the bully with the bully,” he told the judge, who, legend has it, laughed so hard he dismissed all charges and asked instead for a recording. Last Saturday I rode the elevator up with groceries while he carried a case covered in stickers: Save the BW Parkway, This Machine Kills Fascists, Rat Milk Is Vegan. He offered me an apricot from a jar stashed in the cello’s cavity—pitted, soaked in brandy. The fruit dissolved on my tongue like early sunlight. I almost forgave him. I still bang on the ceiling with a broom when the clock edges past midnight because ritual is ritual, and forgiveness loves contrast.

Earlier this year I attended an exhibit on extinct alphabets. One vitrine housed clay tablets from an island that sunk before Christ and now survives only in the fracture patterns of pottery shown under ultraviolet light. Small notches, perhaps numbers, perhaps jokes about unfaithful spouses whose bones look identical under X-rays. Another case presented semaphore flags from a supposed utopian commune where all disagreements were choreographed into dance until the soil gave up on food. A curator explained in a side-video that the group’s final argument involved whether to rotate the hills three degrees south; historical consensus is the earth turned away first. I stared at the remains until security asked me to move along; apparently experiencing existential vertigo at too slow a per-minute clip constitutes loitering.

I left wondering what my own untranslated symbols would look like centuries hence. A chipped enamel pin reading “No Kings, No Landlords”? A grocery list including “pomegranates, cat litter, love”? I imagine future archaeologists holding these signs under glass, arguing: “Clearly pomegranates were currency; the third item must be metaphor.” And some awkward intern will suggest, “Maybe love was literal,” and everyone will laugh because hearts are chemically impractical archaeological evidence.

There is comfort in useless department stores after closing. I discovered this when insomnia kicked me out of bed at 2 a.m. and I rode night buses to the 24-hour superfloors where mannequins wear technicolor trenches no daylight customer dares. I roamed aisles outlined by security cameras humming om-like lullabies. Every perfume nozzles sampled itself into the air every fifteen minutes. I stood transfixed by racks of discounted summer dresses stacked like fleeting monarch wings in plastic. How brave the colors remain under muzak nirvana. I tried on a neon trench too costly to justify even at 70 percent off; in the mirror I looked like a lighthouse that had misplaced its coastline. I left the tag intact but hugged myself for one conspiratorial instant against fabric bright enough to pulse. Sometimes borrowing brightness is the closest we come to smuggling dawn past internal customs.

Upon returning home near 4 a.m., I discovered a moth redecorating the ceiling light, dusk dust spiraling onto the futon like snow made of last summer’s coats. I turned off the lamp to spare the insect’s wings, then curled in darkness wondering how many bodies it takes to make a night feel full. The kettle had cooled; I reheated, added cardamom, thought about unreturned text messages bleeding blue into phone screens nationwide. Sipped. Waited.

Sunrise does not always begin in the east. Sometimes it starts inside the hollow at the base of your throat when you wake certain you have just heard your name spoken by no mouth known. Electric grid still incubating under gratitude brownouts. I have taken to leaving voice memos to myself in minor languages I barely learned in college. Romanian one day, Khmer the next. Each greeting is a form of籍贯 relocation; within two syllables I become provisional citizen of elsewhere. Maybe in one of those tonal futures I managed to outrun foreclosure, disappointment, suburban cul-de-sacs. Playback distorts vowels like reflected smog, but the yearning translates. That is enough.

On balance I own too many black T-shirts to be spontaneously invited to color runs, but last May someone on the crew team for a community garden handed me neon bandanas, claimed participation banishes back-pain for 364 days and besides “grief is pigmentary.” To my surprise I paid the registration fee with the same card I’d once maxed out therapy copays on. The afternoon smelled of corn syrup aerosol and dust granting its final absolution. Thirty meters in, an over-enthusiastic ten-year-old lobbed cornstarch raspberry at my solar plexus; I inhaled magenta. When I coughed, sunsets poured out. Every radio-friendly anthem played at mismatching BPM. By the end of 5K I resembled a painting dropped from ten flights. Strangers passed me juice boxes like penance, cups translucent pink as baby aspirin. I posted no pictures. The bruises lasted two weeks. Worth it.

I wanted to tell the garden coordinator afterward that I used to fear pigment because once a lover’s mother said brighter hues were “violent on the sightlines.” Ten years ago I wore monochrome armor to family dinners as a conditional surrender. But watching neighbors dance in a simulacrum of holi curated by municipal parks suppliers, I understood forgery can still be a gateway drug to actual joy if the body craves entry enough. Sometimes approximation heals in advance of truth.

There are bridges whose entire purpose is to dampen the wind. Saw one once on the outskirts of Portland—an engineering marvel named Tilikum Crossing, cables strung like harp staves across the Willamette. Pedestrians, bikes, buses, trams all sharing the same corridor, private vehicles banned. I walked it at magic hour when silver water mirrored indigo and the skyline tilted drunken sideways. Halfway across a small boy darted ahead, dropped a toy submarine. Plastic propellers chipped against railing. Father behind yelled not in anger but concern too soft for the kid to parse over engine whoosh. I picked up the submarine, handed it back. Kid grinned sideways—the particular alignment where milk teeth still equal possibility. We both watched green light begin its algorithmic sweep. I realized I was crying without sound. Trains call it cathode baptism; I call it gravity correcting uneven grief.

Home again, I stuff the subway map from that afternoon between pages of a Rilke book. Paper cupped around paper, fusion unadvertised. I do this with museum passes, tram stubs, protest flyers, prescription receipts—oven-safe layering. Eventually the book bursts at spine like an overstuffed chrysalis. Librarians during my university days told me notations deface, but I believe marginal entropy blesses. Every future reader inherits breadcrumbs like territory markers, becomes witness to simultaneity where poetry and random Tuesday coexisted inside same vertebra. This is my slow answer to the question nobody asked: How can one alphabet carry more than alphabeting?

Conversely, absence has its own scent. My grandmother stored vanilla pods in empty cigarette boxes post-quitting; drawers that once reeked of menthol past slowly transformed into dessert ghosts. She passed last spring. When the undertaker handed over her sweater, I burrowed my face into the folds expecting eucalyptus or rosewater. Instead I found tobacco reborn into bakery planetarium. I drove home convinced memory transmutes the way wormholes bend light—distortion and revelation braided.

The other day I googled “longest recorded silence.” A webpage claimed it’s 39 minutes between movements in a John Cage performance, audience conditioned to sit in hush amplifying untrained ears. I wondered what resides inside personal silences that exceed hours. I once dated a man who could swallow an entire argument into his cheeks until dimples became fault lines. Silence between us grew to months, finally years. Outlived the relationship itself. Sometimes a name surfaces, and I remember: that yearning never reached arrest, just exile.

Midnight now plus one. The city’s towers blow out individual bulbs, the way parents turn off closet lights one bulb at bedtime. The kettle reaches crescendo again. I refill the chipped mug, let chamomile speak in steam hieroglyphics. Somewhere outside a night bus exhales diesel perfume. Inside, the succulents have agreed not to sue for negligence tonight; they respire in peace.

The cat-shaped hole in the corridor still mews especially when quiet greens the edges. I don’t leave food anymore. Some nights I whisper names I never pressed onto fur in person in case the echo wants one. All grief could be recursion—the body asks back what it gave.

Tonight the sky is clear enough to see satellite float like manufactured stars. I wave. Who doesn’t wag a hand toward machines they paid taxes for, bidden nightly to keep mapping, relay, consolidate bandwidth so we can watch videos of strangers telling us we’re enough? I once wished on one of these glints mid-breakup. The relationship ended anyway but, two days later, I found a five-dollar bill folded into a paper crane on the sidewalk. Coincidence? Absolutely. That’s why I kept the wings somewhere safe.

I am almost at word-count surplus, and the kettle has begun its third act. I take that as cue. Last swallow of tea. Final exhale. I leave some marrow on the page because bones belong to seasons ahead. Perhaps when next insomnia demands tribute, I’ll check in to see how much of tonight’s steam survived translation. Until then, let the moths keep drafting treaties with the ceiling light. Let them teach me how to orbit something incandescent without burning up, without burning out.

Everyone else avoided that corner, so I carried my basket beneath itature’s stage lighting and read the labels on soap bottles like they were koans: Cold Water Protects Darker Colors. It Feels Like Sunday. Who invents these mantras? I picture a room of failed poets now sublimating grief through branding: the ultimate workshop where each stanza is pre-approved for allergen-free earth-impact market metrics.

Eventually the benefactor’s quarter cavalry ran out with three minutes left. Socks still damp, I fed two of my own coins because fabric softener commercials insist life is unbearable without inner plush. This is how capitalism channels tenderness: make tenderness impossible to afford, then sell it back at low APR.

Back upstairs, the alley cats are quarreling in minor keys. Their yowls carry through thin plaster like soprano circular saws. I pinch the spiral notebook in which I jot sentences deemed unsocialized enough for any public forum: “I sleep better now that I no longer believe the sky is a ceiling.” Or: “My mirror keeps asking to be forgiven for reflecting yesterday without consent.” They sit patient between grocery lists and the specs for friend texted me for a raspberry-pi home fermentation monitor. Tiny revolutions under a rubber-band siege.

Once, I tried to mail my future self a letter—part time-capsule, part apology. I wrote on onion paper, sealed it in wax marbled with turmeric, went to the post office where the woman behind the counter informed me the furthest future delivery offered was six months. Imagine: even futurity has bureaucratic limits. I filled out the yellow form, paid twelve quarters, and walked out feeling like I had arranged my own funeral rehearsal. Six months later, on a Tuesday indistinguishable from the others, the letter arrived while I hunched over the toilet lid replacing the screws. I slit the seal with a screwdriver. Past-me had written, sincerely, to forgive Present-me for whatever debts remained after late-stage capitalism implodes. Reading that almost broke me in a good way, like the time I found polyester frogs stitched to childhood pajama pants in a cousin’s attic, still smelling faintly of bubble gum and pine sap. We look to evidence that time can in fact be non-linear; sometimes it accommodates through paper enzymes and scented permanence.

Speaking of permanence: the tenant above me murders out-of-tune cellos at 11:42 p.m. every night. He claims the building’s landlord allows it because he is curing himself of “cello-anxiety,” a term he invented during a Zoom court hearing for noise complaints. “Confront the bully with the bully,” he told the judge, who, legend has it, laughed so hard he dismissed all charges and asked instead for a recording. Last Saturday I rode the elevator up with groceries while he carried a case covered in stickers: Save the BW Parkway, This Machine Kills Fascists, Rat Milk Is Vegan. He offered me an apricot from a jar stashed in the cello’s cavity—pitted, soaked in brandy. The fruit dissolved on my tongue like early sunlight. I almost forgave him. I still bang on the ceiling with a broom when the clock edges past midnight because ritual is ritual, and forgiveness loves contrast.

Earlier this year I attended an exhibit on extinct alphabets. One vitrine housed clay tablets from an island that sunk before Christ and now survives only in the fracture patterns of pottery shown under ultraviolet light. Small notches, perhaps numbers, perhaps jokes about unfaithful spouses whose bones look identical under X-rays. Another case presented semaphore flags from a supposed utopian commune where all disagreements were choreographed into dance until the soil gave up on food. A curator explained in a side-video that the group’s final argument involved whether to rotate the hills three degrees south; historical consensus is the earth turned away first. I stared at the remains until security asked me to move along; apparently experiencing existential vertigo at too slow a per-minute clip constitutes loitering.

I left wondering what my own untranslated symbols would look like centuries hence. A chipped enamel pin reading “No Kings, No Landlords”? A grocery list including “pomegranates, cat litter, love”? I imagine future archaeologists holding these signs under glass, arguing: “Clearly pomegranates were currency; the third item must be metaphor.” And some awkward intern will suggest, “Maybe love was literal,” and everyone will laugh because hearts are chemically impractical archaeological evidence.

There is comfort in useless department stores after closing. I discovered this when insomnia kicked me out of bed at 2 a.m. and I rode night buses to the 24-hour superfloors where mannequins wear technicolor trenches no daylight customer dares. I roamed aisles outlined by security cameras humming om-like lullabies. Every perfume nozzles sampled itself into the air every fifteen minutes. I stood transfixed by racks of discounted summer dresses stacked like fleeting monarch wings in plastic. How brave the colors remain under muzak nirvana. I tried on a neon trench too costly to justify even at 70 percent off; in the mirror I looked like a lighthouse that had misplaced its coastline. I left the tag intact but hugged myself for one conspiratorial instant against fabric bright enough to pulse. Sometimes borrowing brightness is the closest we come to smuggling dawn past internal customs.

Upon returning home near 4 a.m., I discovered a moth redecorating the ceiling light, dusk dust spiraling onto the futon like snow made of last summer’s coats. I turned off the lamp to spare the insect’s wings, then curled in darkness wondering how many bodies it takes to make a night feel full. The kettle had cooled; I reheated, added cardamom, thought about unreturned text messages bleeding blue into phone screens nationwide. Sipped. Waited.

Sunrise does not always begin in the east. Sometimes it starts inside the hollow at the base of your throat when you wake certain you have just heard your name spoken by no mouth known. Electric grid still incubating under gratitude brownouts. I have taken to leaving voice memos to myself in minor languages I barely learned in college. Romanian one day, Khmer the next. Each greeting is a form of籍贯 relocation; within two syllables I become provisional citizen of elsewhere. Maybe in one of those tonal futures I managed to outrun foreclosure, disappointment, suburban cul-de-sacs. Playback distorts vowels like reflected smog, but the yearning translates. That is enough.

On balance I own too many black T-shirts to be spontaneously invited to color runs, but last May someone on the crew team for a community garden handed me neon bandanas, claimed participation banishes back-pain for 364 days and besides “grief is pigmentary.” To my surprise I paid the registration fee with the same card I’d once maxed out therapy copays on. The afternoon smelled of corn syrup aerosol and dust granting its final absolution. Thirty meters in, an over-enthusiastic ten-year-old lobbed cornstarch raspberry at my solar plexus; I inhaled magenta. When I coughed, sunsets poured out. Every radio-friendly anthem played at mismatching BPM. By the end of 5K I resembled a painting dropped from ten flights. Strangers passed me juice boxes like penance, cups translucent pink as baby aspirin. I posted no pictures. The bruises lasted two weeks. Worth it.

I wanted to tell the garden coordinator afterward that I used to fear pigment because once a lover’s mother said brighter hues were “violent on the sightlines.” Ten years ago I wore monochrome armor to family dinners as a conditional surrender. But watching neighbors dance in a simulacrum of holi curated by municipal parks suppliers, I understood forgery can still be a gateway drug to actual joy if the body craves entry enough. Sometimes approximation heals in advance of truth.

There are bridges whose entire purpose is to dampen the wind. Saw one once on the outskirts of Portland—an engineering marvel named Tilikum Crossing, cables strung like harp staves across the Willamette. Pedestrians, bikes, buses, trams all sharing the same corridor, private vehicles banned. I walked it at magic hour when silver water mirrored indigo and the skyline tilted drunken sideways. Halfway across a small boy darted ahead, dropped a toy submarine. Plastic propellers chipped against railing. Father behind yelled not in anger but concern too soft for the kid to parse over engine whoosh. I picked up the submarine, handed it back. Kid grinned sideways—the particular alignment where milk teeth still equal possibility. We both watched green light begin its algorithmic sweep. I realized I was crying without sound. Trains call it cathode baptism; I call it gravity correcting uneven grief.

Home again, I stuff the subway map from that afternoon between pages of a Rilke book. Paper cupped around paper, fusion unadvertised. I do this with museum passes, tram stubs, protest flyers, prescription receipts—oven-safe layering. Eventually the book bursts at spine like an overstuffed chrysalis. Librarians during my university days told me notations deface, but I believe marginal entropy blesses. Every future reader inherits breadcrumbs like territory markers, becomes witness to simultaneity where poetry and random Tuesday coexisted inside same vertebra. This is my slow answer to the question nobody asked: How can one alphabet carry more than alphabeting?

Conversely, absence has its own scent. My grandmother stored vanilla pods in empty cigarette boxes post-quitting; drawers that once reeked of menthol past slowly transformed into dessert ghosts. She passed last spring. When the undertaker handed over her sweater, I burrowed my face into the folds expecting eucalyptus or rosewater. Instead I found tobacco reborn into bakery planetarium. I drove home convinced memory transmutes the way wormholes bend light—distortion and revelation braided.

The other day I googled “longest recorded silence.” A webpage claimed it’s 39 minutes between movements in a John Cage performance, audience conditioned to sit in hush amplifying untrained ears. I wondered what resides inside personal silences that exceed hours. I once dated a man who could swallow an entire argument into his cheeks until dimples became fault lines. Silence between us grew to months, finally years. Outlived the relationship itself. Sometimes a name surfaces, and I remember: that yearning never reached arrest, just exile.

Midnight now plus one. The city’s towers blow out individual bulbs, the way parents turn off closet lights one bulb at bedtime. The kettle reaches crescendo again. I refill the chipped mug, let chamomile speak in steam hieroglyphics. Somewhere outside a night bus exhales diesel perfume. Inside, the succulents have agreed not to sue for negligence tonight; they respire in peace.

The cat-shaped hole in the corridor still mews especially when quiet greens the edges. I don’t leave food anymore. Some nights I whisper names I never pressed onto fur in person in case the echo wants one. All grief could be recursion—the body asks back what it gave.

Tonight the sky is clear enough to see satellite float like manufactured stars. I wave. Who doesn’t wag a hand toward machines they paid taxes for, bidden nightly to keep mapping, relay, consolidate bandwidth so we can watch videos of strangers telling us we’re enough? I once wished on one of these glints mid-breakup. The relationship ended anyway but, two days later, I found a five-dollar bill folded into a paper crane on the sidewalk. Coincidence? Absolutely. That’s why I kept the wings somewhere safe.

I am almost at word-count surplus, and the kettle has begun its third act. I take that as cue. Last swallow of tea. Final exhale. I leave some marrow on the page because bones belong to seasons ahead. Perhaps when next insomnia demands tribute, I’ll check in to see how much of tonight’s steam survived translation. Until then, let the moths keep drafting treaties with the ceiling light. Let them teach me how to orbit something incandescent without burning up, without burning out.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
